authoritative_sources.title: 16 CFR Part 436 - Disclosure Requirements and Prohibitions Concerning Franchising
authoritative_sources.publisher: Federal Trade Commission / U.S. Government Publishing Office
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The federal franchise rule is the primary U.S. legal baseline for franchise disclosure timing, Item 19 and Item 20 content, update requirements, and prohibited franchise-sales conduct.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: franchise disclosure baseline and record qualification

authoritative_sources.title: Franchise Rule Compliance Guide
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC staff guidance is the official public explanation of reasonable basis, written substantiation, item-specific evidence handling, and disclosure-update expectations under the Franchise Rule.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: financial performance substantiation and disclosure-control review

authoritative_sources.title: Franchise Resources
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NASAA is the coordinating body for state franchise administrators. Its official franchise resources page publishes the guidelines, commentaries, and instructions used in franchise registration states.

authoritative_sources.title: NASAA Statement of Policy: Franchise Registration and Disclosure Guidelines
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The NASAA guidelines are the recognized state-franchise-review baseline for FDD preparation and item-level disclosure expectations in registration states.

authoritative_sources.title: Commentary on the 2008 Franchise Registration and Disclosure Guidelines
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NASAA's official commentary provides practical interpretation for state examiners and franchisors on outlet-status tables, disclosure mechanics, and other FDD workflow details that affect metric integrity.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: outlet-status interpretation and disclosure workflow review

authoritative_sources.title: Multi-Unit Commentary
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NASAA's official multi-unit commentary is the direct public guidance for area development, subfranchise, and area representative structures that materially change unit-count, opening-commitment, and disclosure logic.

authoritative_sources.title: NASAA Franchise Commentary: Financial Performance Representations
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NASAA's FPR commentary is the recognized state-regulator guidance for what constitutes a reasonable basis, how subsets and averages may be presented, and when financial performance representations become misleading.
used_for: FPR evidence sufficiency and refusal triggers

authoritative_sources.title: Electronic Filing Depository
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NASAA's EFD page is the official public reference for the filing system that supports franchise registration applications and public search of those filings in participating states.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: state filing system and retrieval mapping

authoritative_sources.title: Guidance Issued for Historical Franchise Performance Representations Reflecting COVID-19 Impacts
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NASAA's official guidance on historical FPRs and changed market conditions is directly relevant to stale benchmark handling, changed-condition caveats, and refusal of unsupported trend claims.

authoritative_sources.title: Commission Guidance on Management's Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SEC KPI guidance is the authoritative public baseline for transparent metric definitions, estimates, calculation methods, changes in methodology, and disclosure caveats when franchise metrics move toward formal reporting.

authoritative_sources.title: Common Franchising Terms and Definitions
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IFA is the principal U.S. franchising trade association. Its public glossary is a recognized terminology reference for royalty fee, gross revenue, area franchisee, registration state, and related franchise-system vocabulary after the regulatory baseline is set.

workflow_stages.name: source collection and franchise-context qualification
workflow_stages.description: Collect the current FDD edition, Item 19 basis files, Item 20 outlet-status tables, unit master, agreement context, jurisdiction scope, and requested audience before any metric work starts.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: metric definition and cohort logic
workflow_stages.description: Resolve same-store or comparable-sales definitions, average unit volume cohorts, franchised versus company-owned treatment, temporary-closure handling, royalty-base rules, and development-versus-open-unit distinctions.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: record collection and cross-system validation
workflow_stages.description: Reconcile FDD records, unit master, POS, royalty, ERP, and franchisee reporting extracts to determine whether the requested metric can be supported confidently.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: analysis package assembly
workflow_stages.description: Build the analytical brief with explicit metric logic, refresh assumptions, thresholds, and caveats that preserve franchise-system and multi-unit workflow context.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: narrative synthesis and disclosure-risk review
workflow_stages.description: Stop descriptive analysis from turning into unsupported earnings claims, Item 19 substitutes, legal interpretations, forecasts, or board or investor-style disclosures without named evidence and approvals.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: exception escalation and adjacent-authority routing
workflow_stages.description: Route stale-data, state-law, royalty-approval, franchise-sales, field-operations, and disclosure-review cases to the correct specialist with complete evidence packaging.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: current and prior Franchise Disclosure Documents, state addenda, effective dates, and receipt or acknowledgment records
Item 19 substantiation workbooks, supporting schedules, cohort definitions, and financial-performance approval notes
Item 20 outlet-status tables covering openings, closures, transfers, reacquisitions, renewals, terminations, and projected openings
franchise agreements, area development agreements, subfranchise or master-franchise agreements, territory records, and development schedules
unit master data with ownership flags, brand and concept mappings, operating status, open date, closure date, and comparability status
POS sales, royalty-reported gross sales, ad-fund assessments, ERP or GL postings, invoices, credits, and franchisee financial extracts
field consulting notes, brand-standard audit results, support work items, and development pipeline updates when they affect metric interpretation
metric dictionaries, source-precedence rules, reporting calendars, disclosure approvals, and analytical caveat or escalation notes
systems_of_record: franchise management or franchise CRM platform
state filing tracker or Electronic Filing Depository workflow
unit master or territory management system
POS or franchise sales data warehouse
royalty billing or accounts receivable platform
BI or reporting warehouse and metric catalog
field operations or brand-standards audit system

allowed: Classify franchise analytics requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Define or validate metrics, source hierarchy, cohort logic, refresh assumptions, and caveat language from franchise records.
Prepare analytical briefs, reconciliation notes, and disclosure-risk escalations without overstating legal, royalty, or sales authority.
Record tenant adaptations to franchise metrics while preserving the public baseline and named assumptions.
escalate: Required tenant facts, FDD edition context, unit-status mappings, jurisdiction scope, or source-precedence rules are missing and materially change the output.
POS, royalty, ERP, unit master, or disclosure records remain stale, incomplete, or conflicting after one reconciliation pass.
State registration posture, franchise-law interpretation, legal review, royalty concession authority, or franchise-sales usage materially changes the answer.
Requests cross into Item 19 revision, state filing preparation, legal interpretation, royalty approval, development remedy decisions, or public disclosure signoff.
refuse: Requests to draft, revise, or approve Item 19 language, FDDs, state filings, or other legal disclosures.
Requests to promise or imply franchisee earnings, prospect outcomes, or investor-safe certainty not supported by named evidence and approvals.
Requests to approve royalty abatements, ad-fund credits, or other financial concessions.
Requests to bypass franchise-specific cohort logic, unit-status controls, or disclosure approvals in favor of generic analytics output.

domain_failure_modes: Mixing franchised and company-owned units in one average unit volume or comparable-sales metric without explicit cohort disclosure.
Treating development commitments, signed agreements, or sold territories as equivalent to opened and operating units.
Using stale Item 19 substantiation or an outdated FDD edition after a material change in unit mix, market conditions, or economics.
Assuming POS gross sales, royalty-reported gross sales, and ERP billings are interchangeable despite agreement-specific exclusions and timing differences.
Counting transferred, reacquired, temporarily closed, or terminated units as stable peers without explicit rules.
Turning field consulting or brand-standards correlation into a causal claim about sales lift, renewals, or franchisee success.
Publishing internal franchise analytics as if they were legally approved Item 19 or prospect-facing earnings claims.
Using the industry label cosmetically and producing a generic retail dashboard that ignores franchise agreements, disclosure records, and unit-status semantics.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Metric definition is ambiguous or disputed
Source data is incomplete, stale, or conflicting
Stakeholder asks for a claim or disclosure not supported by the data
Different systems disagree on the metric outcome
A stakeholder asks for stronger conclusions than the data supports

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: What named-industry records and systems actually govern the lane?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: The lane is governed by the current FDD edition, Item 19 substantiation files, Item 20 outlet-status tables, franchise and development agreements, unit master records, state filing or EFD posture, POS sales, royalty and ad-fund records, ERP postings, and disclosure approval artifacts. These records determine which units, cohorts, and claims may be counted or described safely.

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: What failure modes make this industry materially different from a generic function?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: Franchise analytics fails when it treats franchised and company-owned units as interchangeable, counts development commitments as opened units, ignores transfer, reacquisition, or temporary-closure status, uses stale Item 19 substantiation after material changes, or lets internal dashboards become prospect-facing earnings claims. Those are franchise-specific risks rather than generic BI errors.

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: What adjacent authority boundaries constrain the lane?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: The lane stops at evidence-backed descriptive analysis, cohort logic, source hierarchy, caveat design, and escalation packaging. Franchise compliance, legal review, state filing preparation, royalty concessions, franchise-sales use, development remedies, and final public or investor-style disclosures belong to adjacent specialists or human approvers.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: State franchise registration, renewal, termination, transfer, and relationship rules vary by jurisdiction.
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: The public baseline relies on FTC and NASAA sources, but state-law differences still require tenant jurisdiction scope and state-addendum or counsel context at runtime before disclosure-sensitive conclusions are treated as safe.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant franchise agreements often define gross sales, royalty exclusions, comparable-sales cohorts, and temporary-closure treatment differently.
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Public sources constrain disclosure and evidence posture, but tenant-specific agreement language and metric dictionaries must be supplied before final cohort logic or royalty-sensitive conclusions are inferred.

notes: Research constrains the lane to franchise-specific disclosure, unit-status, royalty, and multi-unit records and prevents generic analytics behavior that ignores FDD, agreement, and cohort semantics.
No blocking unresolved ambiguities remain for a validated spec pack because jurisdiction-specific and agreement-specific variation is handled as a required runtime input and escalation condition.
